chunks.text: Shafi Goldwasser is a Professor at UC Berkeley's EECS department. She serves as Director of the Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing. Her principal fields of interest include Cryptography, Computational Number Theory, Complexity Theory, Fault Tolerant Distributed Computing, Probabilistic Proof Systems, and Approximation Algorithms.

Shafi Goldwasser earned her Ph.D. in Computer Science from UC Berkeley in 1984, after completing an M.S. in Computer Science at UC Berkeley in 1981. She received her B.S. in Mathematics and Science from Carnegie Mellon University in 1979. Her research areas focus on Theory (THY), and she is affiliated with several research centers including the Berkeley Center for Responsible, Decentralized Intelligence (RDI), Center for the Theoretical Foundations of Learning, Inference, Information, Intelligence, Mathematics and Microeconomics at Berkeley (CLIMB), and the Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing (SITC).

Shafi Goldwasser has received numerous prestigious honors and awards throughout her career. Notable recognitions include the ACM A.M. Turing Award in 2012, the Gödel Prize in 1993 and 2001, the Grace Murray Hopper Award in 1996, and election to the National Academy of Sciences in 2004. She is also a Fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) and the International Association for Cryptologic Research (IACR).

Additional honors include the L'Oreal-UNESCO For Women in Science International Award in 2021, Foundations of Computer Science (FOCS) Test of Time Award in 2021, Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC) Test of Time Award in 2021, and the Benjamin Franklin Medal in Computer and Cognitive Science in 2010. She has been recognized with memberships in the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, National Academy of Engineering, and American Academy of Letters and Sciences.
